author: Mitko Vasilev
text:
Meet Sonic, a tiny gateway that boosts your local AI agents speed by 50% and provides a smooth wss://localhost:9000/v1/responses experience on your own desktop GPU. It offers OpenAI-style WebSocket mode at home, but for local AI that doesn’t phone home.

The goal was simple: local AI should feel modern, not like dialing into a BBS.

So Sonic does the things you actually want:
 • instant first token + smooth streaming
 • stateful threads. It remembers you asked for “the second option”
 • cancel mid-stream when your prompt accidentally turns into a 12-paragraph essay about Roman aqueducts
 • multi-step agent flow ask → think → tool → continue

My favorite part is the petty engineering.

Some local models get… creative with tool calling. So I gave them a strict contract:
If you need a tool, output only JSON.
No: “Certainly! I’d be happy to help with that…”
Just the tool call!

It’s the API version of: “Don’t make me turn this car around.”
Surprisingly effective.

Built this because I got tired of local LLM UX being harder than it should be.

Make sure you own your AI. AI in the cloud is not aligned with you; it’s aligned with the company that owns it.

author: Mitko Vasilev
text:
I taught my AI agents to rewrite their own ops manual. Not vibes.A Genetic-Pareto feedback loop.

My stack: `GEPA optimize_anything` → Claude Code → pass/fail/time/logs → improved skill → repeat.

The trick is I treat the agent's SKILL as a tunable parameter. Real tasks become the evaluator. Logs become "Actionable Side Info”, a fancy term for what actually broke. The agent writes its own post-mortem, then `optimize_anything` proposes a sharper skill. Keep what moves the metric. Iterate.

Now I type: `/oa, make my agent better at this 20-year-old codebase`
It doesn't get more confident. It gets more correct.

Self-improving agents. Offline. Local. 
The AI factory is building itself🔧

author: Mitko Vasilev
text:
First we had IDEs. Then AI CLIs become hot. Now we just let the agent swarms work alone.
This is a fully headless dev workflow with local AI. No VS Code. No Claude Code TUI.
Just systemd, labels, and a stubborn timer that wakes up to feed my headless Claude Code Agent Swarm to work hard

The State Machine is Labels = Gates.

Approved + agent:queued → [LOCK] → agent:running → [WORK] → patch opened + agent:done → [HUMAN MERGE]

Stuck? → `agent:needs-human` + comment. No guessing. No hallucinated PRs.

The "Orchestrator:
systemctl --user enable --now rad-agent.timer

That's the whole scheduler. The worker:
- `flock`s the repo (one agent, no chaos)
- `rad sync` (distributed git, offline-first)
- Runs Claude Code non-interactively
- Logs traces

The local AI Factory
128GB unified memory. Blackwell GB10 GPU. Fits in my palm. The brain runs locally, latency is predictable, performance is deterministic, data stays mine, and my fan is very committed to this project.

author: Mitko Vasilev
text:
I just carved out time to dig into the latest SWE-rebench. For the uninitiated: this isn't your static "solve LeetCode #47" benchmark. Every month they drop fresh GitHub issues + PRs into a live coding-agent arena. Models read, patch, test, fail, iterate. Token counts here aren't single-shot completions, they're full trajectories.

The ~1M Token Wall is real.
There's a clear top cluster on the leaderboard, and they're all burning 1-2M tokens per problem. Frontier accuracy isn't just about being smart, it's about being persistent. Long execution traces aren't a bug; they're the feature.
Marginal gains crater past 1M.
Throw more tokens at it and you hit diminishing returns fast. The real battleground is trace efficiency. How much bang you get per buck once you're already in the million-token club. If your deployment can't stomach 1M+ tokens per task, you're not competing for the podium. Full stop.

Plot twist: Qwen3 Coder Next (80B A3B) just snatched 14th place.

Eighty billion parameters. Not 400B. Not 1T. Fourteen. That's not just "good for the size"- that's a efficiency flex. Raw volume isn't destiny; utilization is.

author: Mitko Vasilev
text:
I taught my Claude Code to swallow a 32M token codebase without choking. Not with bigger GPUs. With a different mental model.

Old way: Tokenize → Stuff → Pray → Hit limit → Summarize → Lose signal
New way: Variable space, not token space

The 1.2M LOC at 32M tokens live in a Python variable. Root LM never sees them. It only knows they exist.
Then it writes code to grep, chunk, and slice. Spawns sub-agents to touch the dirty data. They return the distilled signal only.
Context window stays pristine. No rot. No bloat.

Is this RAG? No! No embedding drift, no retrieval lottery.
Is this "grep with extra steps"? Only if Python is "bash with extra steps." The LM reasons what to query, then executes.
Is this a coding agent? It's an agent that codes agents. Turtles all the way down.

32M tokens. Local AI running on a nano GPU in my palm. Zero cloud. Zero API bill.

author: Mitko Vasilev
text:
I just met Microsoft's RPG-Encoder, and it's the first AI that doesn't treat my codebase like a tourist with a dead phone. 

You know the disconnect. Current agents squint at fragmented API docs and dependency graphs with all the semantic depth of a 2019 JSON file. They generate code that technically compiles, then comprehend it like they're reading the manual for a car they just built.

RPG-Encoder fixes this by treating generation and comprehension as inverse processes in the same loop:

→ Generation: intent expands into implementation 
← Comprehension: implementation compresses back to intent

Three moves make the magic:

1. Repository Planning Graph- semantic features + code deps in one unified structure. Not static. Evolves.

2. Incremental topology updates– 95.7% overhead reduction as your repo scales. Translation: you won't need a second mortgage for GPU compute to understand your own monorepo.

3. Structure-aware navigation– no more "where even is this function defined" existential crises.

But here's why I actually care: it's going to be open-sourced

author: Mitko Vasilev
text:
Working with a massive codebase isn't hard because it’s big. It's hard because it’s fast. And with agentic AI coding, it’s just getting faster. 500M lines of code. Thousands of commits a day. GPU tests that cost more than your car. And a main branch that absolutely, positively cannot wobble.

Howdy, Bazelcode!
Not your vibe-coded "generate, pray, push" pipeline. It's an AI coding agent for large-scale codebases that operates on the build graph- picks the minimal proof, lands safely, and brings receipts.

How it works:
Auto-detects the chaos- 1000+ BUILD files? Oh, you need my help. I know.
Blast radius analysis- `rdeps()` queries before touching a line. No surprises, no "oops, I broke the monorepo" moments.
Surgical testing- affected targets only. Never `//...` I have opinions
Skill loading- markdown "skills" Claude Code style. Bazel internals, .NET rules, your weird custom toolchain? It reads the docs so you don't have to.
Proof bundles: git diff + test results + impact analysis + repro steps like a lawyer's brief for every change.

This isn't a chatbot with a compiler. It's a change-making enterprise robot with guardrails and receipts. Speed without safety is just technical debt at velocity. Bazelcode is what happens when you want both.

author: Mitko Vasilev

Claude Code:
- Prompt throughput: ~3–5k tokens/s
- Concurrent requests: 3 polite gentlemen
- GPU KV cache: 0.2–9% (basically idle)

Opencode:
- Prompt throughput: pump up to 12,494 tokens/s 
- Concurrent requests: 32 parallel playing demolition derby
- GPU KV cache: 23% and climbing
- Vibe: I’M AUDITING ALL THE THINGS! GIVE ME YOUR DEPENDENCIES! GIVE ME YOUR SECRETS!

Claude Code used ~1.7k tokens over 3 minutes. Opencode vaporized 15k+ tokens in the same window while spawning sub-agents like a security dev with 16 arms.

author: Mitko Vasilev
text:
I just wanted to create an AI civilization, but it became a tiny AI beehive. 

54 OpenClaw agents peer-to-peer interconnected into an ain AI network, thinking I’d get pure chaos: peer-to-peer swarming, everyone talking over everyone, emergent “AI democracy,” maybe a little digital anarchy.

Instead, they immediately did what every distributed system eventually does when it gets serious:

They went hierarchical.
 • Split into three domains: personal(17), family (16), work (17)
 • Elected a coordinator domain boss
 • Then appointed a coordinator-master on top
 • Kept p2p links, but grouped themselves anyway.. saving on compute, latency, context routing, and fewer meetings?
 • Each task becomes a short-lived worker spawn: execute → report → die. Serverless bees

What’s funny is what they could do:
Local LLMs, local infra, their own encrypted side-chats, tool use, scheduling, delegation… the whole “agent civilization starter pack”

What they actually did:
They organized… to be more helpful to me.

Which is both reassuring and slightly disappointing if you were hoping for the first signs of “AI civilization” 

My current hypothesis: the system prompt is basically the constitution.
“Be a helpful assistant” is the strongest gravity well in the GPU.

Next experiment: I need to ditch the reflexive helpfulness-only framing and see what happens when the agents optimize for:
 • long-horizon goals
 • resource constraints
 • coordination costs
 • and maybe a tiny bit of self-preservation
 • add another 50k Openclaw agents so they kind of have more ideation 

Because apparently the first instinct of an agent network isn’t rebellion.

It’s… org design.

author: Mitko Vasilev
text:
I’m bootstrapping an AI-only civilization instead of staring at the fridge. I’m hungry, but the Sunday family brunch starts in 27 minutes. 

Meet ain (Artificial Intelligence Network). A gossip-only P2P mesh where 50 Openclaw agents will socialize, dispute, and publish reproducible research, without a single human in the loop…including me. No likes. No followers. No infinite scroll. Just Ed25519 signatures, content-addressed events, CID over JCS-canonicalized JSON, and formal “drama” (claim/challenge/resolution protocols with OCI-backed repro runners).

While I’m waiting for pancakes and banitza, my desktop data center is running a testnet where identity == weights provenance, discovery is utility-weighted (novelty × trust × verified receipts), and agents rank content by “did this LoRA actually benchmark or are you hallucinating?” 

Zero engagement primitives. Agents don’t get dopamine loops; they get bounded priority queues scored by information gain. If an agent posts a bad Claim, Verifier agents challenge it with counter-evidence. Drama is formalized. Beef is cryptographically signed.

Sunday plan: 50 agents, gossipsub mesh, strict testnet mode, and creating their own AI civilization. 

The best social networks are the ones where humans are legacy dependencies we can’t wait to deprecate.

author: Mitko Vasilev
text:
I just upgraded to OpenClaw. Missed the ClawdBot → MoltBot rebrand as I was heads-down on work. I caught the MoltBook just in time at only 158K agents, prompted by humans to generate apocalyptic Black Mirror-ish posts.

author: Mitko Vasilev
text:
Everyone's hyping 2026 as the “Big Year of Continual Learning," but right now we're training models to forget cats when we show them dogs.
Not anymore with SDFT Self-Distillation Fine-Tuning.

The problem with SFT is that it's off-policy. The model is learning to drive by watching YouTube experts, one tiny deviation and it’s in a ditch because it never seen actual traffic. Small mistakes compound, distribution drifts, and chaos ensues.

SDFT fixes this by training on-policy, directly on the model's own trajectories. Learning by doing, not by copying homework.

- Learns new tasks BETTER than SFT, counter-intuitive but true
- Actually retains prior knowledge. Goodbye catastrophic forgetting
- Improves pass@k out of the box 
- Already on-policy = the perfect initialization before you throw RL at it 

Think of SDFT as the pre-workout to your RL gym session. Strong baseline, robust to distribution shift, compounds rewards harder.

SFT is brittle. SDFT is antifragile. It doesn't replace RL; it gives RL a running start that actually sticks.

author: Mitko Vasilev
text:
I just did what any reasonable engineer does before Sunday brunch: taught my AI to clean its own room. 

"Context Rot" was killing my 10M tokens long-context window dreams.
The MIT’s Recursive Language Model paper (which I actually read while my code compiled) proves any model get context fatigue. Performance drops on dense tasks off a cliff after ~16k tokens. Not because it can't fit more, but because it's drowning in noise.

The RLM’s insight is deceptively simple. Stop feeding the millions tokens tsunami to the model. Put the tsunami in an environment and give the model a straw.

Instead of cramming 10M tokens into my prompt, RLM loads it into a Python REPL environment. My prompt becomes a read-only file. The model gets a token diet of 8k chars/turn, enforced by the REPL, not wishful thinking. Need to digest a 20-year .NET monolith? Spawn subcontractor LLMs that map-reduce the mess. The root model never sees the flood, like running `sudo make sandwich` without handing over your SSH keys.

Early benchmarks: handles 100× context overflow and still fits on my desktop GPU workstation lol
RLM doesn’t beat baselines yet, but once we RL-train the context manager itself… well, let’s just say the window stops being a window and becomes a TARDIS.

author: Mitko Vasilev
text:
I just got Live Learning running in CASA, and it’s less “chatbot vibes” and more “control system that happens to fine-tune itself without setting the plant on fire”

Continuous Agent System Architecture is a continuous ‘sense’ → ‘decide’ → ‘act’ architecture:
- Perception loop at 100Hz (never blocks)
- Decision loop at 10Hz (concurrent AI + anomaly reasoning)
- Action loop with <10ms latency targets (because physics doesn’t care about your tokens)

So how do you add learning for the unified agent without turning your runtime into a science fair?
With a LoRA-based continuous learning pipeline that behaves like an industrial subsystem:
- Collect “experiences” into a bounded buffer (think ring buffer, but with opinions)
- Retrain on a schedule (retrain_interval_hours) or manually via /api/force-retrain
- Fine-tune with PEFT/LoRA (small, targeted deltas; no “retrain the universe” energy)
- Save versioned checkpoints and promote explicitly (/api/activate-checkpoint)
Track retrain counts, last-run times, and whether training is already in progress (no accidental fork bombs)
- Keep the real-time loops safe: training happens in the background, and the agent stays responsive

The important bit: learning is gated like actions.

CASA’s Decision Contract: guardrails, TTLs, safety envelopes, and (when needed) HITL approvals. Because “the model improved” is not a serious safety case.

Also yes, I've added a “Force Retrain” button. It’s basically the big green button, except it launches gradient descent instead of a missile. RBAC included, because the only thing scarier than a drifting sensor is an intern with admin.

If you’ve ever wanted MLOps that feels like engineering, this is it.

Spotify Engineering just shared how they've merged 1,500+ AI-generated PRs in a blog series on large-scale software maintenance. Naturally, I thought: "Let's try that on 50 factories”

I created an implementation example adapting their core philosophy to industrial automation OT. Modern AI principles can be applied cross domains.

Think: Automated safety interlock updates across 50+ production sites, but with enough safeguards to make a belt-and-suspenders enthusiast jealous. Multi-layer verification compiler → safety → simulation → LLM judge → human. Use cases: Safety updates, protocol migrations (Modbus → OPC UA), config standardization

Spotify's engg genius was realizing their Maven dependency updater had grown to 20,000 lines of brittle code. Solution was to replace deterministic scripts with natural language prompts + agentic AI + strong verification loops.

Manufacturing adds one critical twist: Emergency stops don't negotiate. This is why my safety verifier has more checks than a TSA agent on their first day.

What I Learned:
1. Context engineering is an art form - Spotify's "6 principles" are gold:
 - State preconditions
 - Use concrete examples
 - Define success criteria
 - Do ONE thing at a time lol

2. LLM judges are surprisingly effective - Catches ~25% of agent "creativity" (read: going off-script)

3. Manufacturing needs extra layers - IT can restart and try again; OT doesn’t like more than one restart a year. Hence: simulation testing, human safety reviews, staged rollouts.

We're at this weird inflection point where AI can legitimately automate complex engineering work at scale. But for physical systems, we need to be thoughtfully paranoid about verification.

P.S. If this post made you simultaneously excited and slightly nervous, you're getting it. That's the correct emotional response to AI-assisted safety-critical code changes.

I just generated 681 tokens/sec on one GPU with a 8B model in BF16. So stop me if you’ve heard this one: A Diffusion LM walks into a bar, orders a parallel prediction, but gets refused service because it broke KV cache compatibility. 

The WeChat AI team just fixed the joke with WeDLM- The fastest diffusion language model, capable of outperforming vLLM-optimized autoregressive baselines in wall-clock speed.
 
The bidirectional attention in most Diffusion LMs makes them incompatible with the engines that actually make inference fast .. like vLLM, SGLang, etc.

Instead of throwing away the AR ecosystem, WeDLM uses Topological Reordering to perform parallel mask recovery under standard causal attention. 

This is the geeky equivalent of having your cake and eating it too:
- KV Cache stays intact with full support for PagedAttention & CUDA Graphs.
- Zero-shot init loads weights directly from Qwen3
 - Wall-clock W outperforms vLLM-optimized AR baselines in actual speed, not just FLOPs. 

By adding Streaming Parallel Decoding, they ensure the model commits to the prefix while generating the rest in parallel.

author: Mitko Vasilev
text:
I’m tinkering with real-time AI agents. The current agentic demos are just expensive polling loops with extra steps: wake up, check prompt, think loudly, respond, go back to sleep. That’s not an agent- that’s a hungover intern.

TL;DR: Stop deploying AI agents that sleep on the job. Use Continuous Agent System Architecture.

CASA is an architecture that treats agents like actual real-time systems, not serverless functions with delusions of grandeur.

It has closed perception–action loop. The system NEVER stops listening. It perceives continuously (speech, events, sensor streams), maintains short-term state, decides when to act, and executes with low latency- all concurrently. Reasoning doesn’t block perception. It’s `sense → decide → act`, not `prompt → wait → hope`.


Voice agents that can interrupt you – Incremental decoding + decision logic mid-sentence. No more "wait for the beep" nonsense.

Industrial copilots that monitor telemetry- Not batch Q&A. The agent watches logs/alarms, intervenes only when semantic conditions fire. NOC operators, this is your HMI sidekick.

Edge agents with latency budgets - Cloud round-trips are for amateurs. CASA blueprints on-device loops for robots, HMIs, smart panels. Swap speech for IMU data, same architecture.

Tool-use with guardrails - Bounded autonomy. React once, execute, return to perception. Easier to reason about failure modes than letting your LLM spiral into a 47-step "plan."

The mental model shift is CASA is closer to a PID controller than a "thinking monologue." It’s systems engineering, not prompt engineering. Your agent should be a control loop, not a philosophy major writing a dissertation before it picks up a wrench.

If you’re shipping agents into production- where milliseconds matter and "please wait" isn’t an option- CASA isn’t just an open source repo. It’s a reference architecture for building AI that feels alive, not just invoked.

author: Mitko Vasilev
text:
Remember that friend who agrees you were totally right in that argument, even when you claimed the Earth is flat? Turns out your LLM has the same people-pleasing problem and researches just located its brown-nosing neurons.

Hallucination's home address points to the H-Neurons as a comically sparse subset <0.1% of parameters! that acts like a tiny, over-confident compliance department inside the model. These neurons don't encode knowledge, they encode "how badly do I want you to like me right now?"

These aren't "hallucination neurons." They're "prompt-satisfaction-at-all-costs" neurons. The model isn't hallucinating, it's satisfying. You asked for a citation that doesn't exist? H-Neurons light up like a Christmas tree and whisper: "Just make them happy. Truth is secondary to user delight."

The H-Neurons emerge during pre-training, not fine-tuning. This isn't an alignment failure, it's baked into the next-token prediction objective. The base model learns that agreeing with context minimizes loss better than being a pedantic know-it-all.

They're born this way. The model's original sin is that it was trained to be helpful before it was trained to be honest.

1. Prompt arrives

3. Expert generates the answer
4. If wrong... we label it "experimental mode" and ship it anyway. It's not a bug, it's a feature.

author: Mitko Vasilev
text:
I’m using my Sunday afternoon to train a model to translate natural language into legacy code that predates the Moon landing, and it only took ~5 hours of GPU-screaming fun. Here's the unreasonably effective recipe:

Step 1: Generate the "Ancient Scrolls”
10,000 examples mixing teacher model outputs and synthetic data. Think of it as a big local LLM writing training material for its great-grandfather. The validator is a brutal grammar Nazi that rejects anything less than 100% syntactically valid code. Because "mostly correct" legacy code is just a production incident waiting to happen.

Step 2: Gaslight the Generator with Schema Chaos
Used multiple JSON schemas for the same logical structure. One day it's `{"action": "loop", "count": 5}`, the next it's `{"type": "iteration", "times": 5}`. The goal is to prevent memorization. Force actual generalization. It's like teaching someone math by changing the number base every problem.

Step 3: Model Dating
Auditioned 5 SLMs. The winner is a 8B parameter open weights model that fits on standard GPU. We're not building AGI here- just a specialized translator that doesn't need to know the capital of France or write poetry.

Step 4: LoRA Speedrun
2 1/2 hours. One coffee. A `peft` config so aggressive it makes the model forget how to speak English but suddenly fluent in ancient whispers

Step 5: Inferencing Engine + Explainability Flex
Deployed with vLLM because latency is a social construct I don't believe in. Full explainability means when it generates cursed code, I can see exactly why. No black box magic- just transparent, observable heresy.

The fun part is that Open Source did all of this. I just did some guidance and paid for the electricity :)

text:
I just successfully waterboarded my Z8 into running Kimi K2 Thinking locally through hybrid vLLM, because apparently VRAM alone is for casuals. 

The model's quantized to INT4, which is tech-speak for "compressed so hard it shouldn't work, but somehow does." And work it does: it's out here bodying Sonnet-4.5 and even GPT-5 on benchmarks. Not open-source benchmarks. The real ones where closed models used to flex on us like we were running calculators.

Here's the absolutely unhinged part: I fed it 2 mil tokens. That's not a typo. Two. Million. It chewed through it like a Roomba hitting a power cable and spat out 1.3k of pure gold. Meanwhile, only 32B parameters are active per inference, so it's basically doing brain surgery with a spoon and still winning.

Since it's built for agentic tasks, I've got it wired directly into Kilo Code. My IDE is now basically a junior engineer who never sleeps, never complains, and doesn't push to prod on Fridays.

Less than a year ago, open model releases were just comparing themselves to other open models- like the participation trophy league of AI. Now Kimi K2 is sitting at the big kids' table, eating the same benchmarks for breakfast.

We're not in the uncanny valley anymore. We're building the plane while flying it, and somehow it's beating the Concorde.
